Wireless tear biosensing systems, including wearable, reusable and non-invasive optical contact lens biosensors, have recently become of interest for diabetes diagnostics (Elsherif et al., 2018b; Yetisen et al., 2014; Emran et al., 2020b).

Elsherif et al. (Kim et al., 2018b) developed optical microscale device-based contact lens glucose sensors for quantitative visualisation assays and POC settings. Microfluidic device visualisation is technically based on the function of periodically ordered photonic structure (PS)-based biosensor building blocks for identification and visible colour tracking of glucose samples. For instance, one-step, fast (5 min) synthesis which includes coating, polymerisation, imprinting PS film, functionalisation of active surface and then attachment to contact lens protocols can control the fabrication of PS contact lens glucose sensors (Scheme 13 ) (Elsherif et al., 2018b). In general, the PS hydrogel sensor is firstly fabricated by the formation of imprinted PS hydrogel film. As shown in Scheme 10A, consequence steps for free-standing manufacture of PS hydrogel glucose detector are achieved via a simple stamping protocol. The imprinted PS process of hydrogel matrix was developed through the modification of photonic stamp of the PS master by a well-ordered monolayer monomer polymer. A hydrogel film was grown under UV polymerisation followed by extraction of the PS master for the replica of the potential PS hydrogel sensor. Then, the incorporation of a PS hydrogel film layer with non-enzyme phenylboronic acid (PBA) provides strong binding with glucose and leads to (i) reversible glucose-to-boronate complex formation in PBS solution and (ii) PS hydrogel swelling. Glucose quantitation can be performed by recording the optical diffraction patterns of the PS hydrogel sensor as a result of the transmission and reflection signalling modes. The read-out sensing principle is based on the glucose concentrations, which significantly affect the swelling/shrinking of the PS hydrogel sensor. The reversible changes in the PS sensor matrix swelling increase the volumetric thickness and periodicity constant of PS sensor films (Elsherif et al., 2018b; Yetisen et al., 2014). The increase in periodicity constant decreases the angle and zero/1st order d-spacing of Bragg peak diffraction profiles according to the glucose-to-boronate binding. The wearable PS hydrogel sensor into contact lens enables suitable response time (in the order of minutes) and visual glucose linearity in the range of 0–50 mM. Furthermore, the integration of sensor lenses into smartphone devise readouts allows non-invasive, short-response detection of glucose within 3 s as a route of diagnostic test at home (Scheme 10D) (Kim et al., 2018b). However, the long-term calibration of wearable contact lens PS hydrogel biosensors is required for clinical diabetic practices (Yetisen et al., 2014).

Considerable sensing optimisation is necessary for sensory devices to offer on-time, personalised monitoring and continuous measurements of glucose and other biomolecules in a swift response (Emran et al., 2018d, 2020b, 2021a, 2021b; Akhtar et al., 2016). Lin et al. reported on visualisation assays of tear glucose level by using portable contact lens sensors for optical and non-invasive diagnostic monitoring (Lin et al., 2018). The fabrication of this contact lens sensor is based on the functional modification of poly-hydroxyethylmethacrylate (poly-HEMA) with PBA. The glucose signal readouts of non-enzymatic PBA-modified poly-HEMA contact lens sensors can be monitored by a real-time image processing program incorporated into a smartphone. The detector red LED light-emitting diode device, in principle, changes the intensity of red light with the changes in the volumetric thickness of the swelled PBA-based poly-HEMA contact lens sensor. The visual set of the diabetic sensing assay is carried out with addition of various tear glucose concentrations (0.1–0.6 mM range) in PBS solutions. As a result of the reversible glucose-to-boronate binding, swelling/shrinking in contact lens sensor thickness simultaneously changes the red LDE light that can be detected and analysed by Sony IMX230 photosensitive image software in a smartphone (Lin et al., 2018). These optical contact lens biosensors offer a simple, visible monitoring of tear glucose, but the integrated wireless digital system based on processing of sensor data acquisition and sensitive transmitting signals in smartphones or over long-distances still have stringent constrains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Remarkable devices for optical tear glucose biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: The recent advancements in optical sensor-based contact lens materials, particularly soft hydrogel lenses-integrated microfluidic chips, provide evidence of simple fabrication, easy-to-use and biocompatible non-enzyme smart polymers. In terms of wearable electronics, optical contact lens sensors may have potential to be fully integrated into portable point-of-care medical devices. As reported in some studies, optical contact lens sensors were not constructed with integrated circuit chipsets, rigid interconnects and external/circumferential power sources to avoid the complicated structures and the distortion of eyelid or even cornea with long-term usage, but their usage in real applicability is still questionable. Regarding sampling collection, the glucose concentrations in tears vary because of irritation or emotion; such phenomenon should be considered to ensure the absence of associated interferences or stimulated environments (Keum et al., 2020).

After close inspection of the significant development of contact lens biosensors, further improvements in reliable fabrications for electronic miniaturisation, signalling and transmission are needed to develop these smart contact lens sensors into on-time point-of-care diagnosis tools. Moreover, visual, soft sensory contact lens allows lab-scale reliability analysis of glucose levels in tears. In turn, contact lens sensors should feature low cost, transparency and comfort, and safety and user friendliness to promote their commercial use (Gu et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Market overview of electronic glucose devices for diabetes
ce:section.ce:para: The current situation of chronic diabetes suggests the urgent need for modern CGM systems to provide personalised and effective healthcare to millions of patients with diabetes. The growth rate of the global market and the latest CGM technology developments have increased. To date, several CGM systems were developed by using flexible, wearable electronic devices to measure glucose levels in the interstitial fluid or blood. Along with these devices, an accurate transmission of the analytical information could be achieved by using a transmitter. In addition, the displaying screen device is essential to improve the reliable CGM of diabetes (El-Safty and Shenashen, 2020a, 2020b; Seshadri et al., 2019). According to CGM market demands, a number of companies have lucrative opportunities for manufacturing wearable and portable glucose devices.

Blood glucose test is usually performed by pricking a patient's finger for blood sample collection onto a disposable test strip (Scheme 14 A) (Bruen et al., 2017; http://technologyinscienc, 2015; https://www.onetouch.com/). The sensor layout is generally composed of layers of electrodes, separators and enzymes frozen in a small package. Recent developments of strip sensor designs are focused in the following: (i) compact size, (ii) auto-sampling collection, (iii) small amount of blood sample and (iv) accurate diabetic examination to reduce disease complications and mortality. Regular collection of blood samples for analysis causes discomfort, pain and stress to patients with diabetes. Virtual developments of portable and sensitive sensors which enable an alternative glucose sampling collection from biological fluids (i.e. sweat, tear and saliva) are an important issue for future healthcare concerns of patients with diabetes to enhance their quality of life.

In this context, the GlucoWatch®G2™ biographer developed a non-invasive glucose analysis with high sensitivity for patients with diabetes (Scheme 14B) [ (Tierney et al., 2001; https://medaval.ie/device). The mechanistic glucose detection by using the GlucoWatch®G2™ biographer device was based on glucose extraction through intact skin via reverse iontophoresis. In addition, the GlucoWatch®G2™ device can provide real-time glucose readings every 20 min for 12 h. This device has three major parts: (i) glucose sensor platform, (ii) transmitter (parts (i) and (ii) are connected to the skin by a patch) and (iii) monitoring screen. The sensor device converts the information data of glucose level in the interstitial fluids into electronic signals, and then the transmitter transfers to the readable monitor at a specific period. Moreover, the iPro2 device controls the screaming, reading and storing of glucose level every 5 min for 6 days (Scheme 14C) (Welsh et al., 2012; https://www.mesm.com/prod). This device features water resistance, light weight and compact size. However, the applications of the iPro2 device are limited by the difficult direct reading of data by patients. Along with the utilization of the iPro2 device, the reading-out process can be obtained using a special software.

To provide real-time data analysis and fast response feedback to patients, the Dexcom G4 PLATINUM Professional device was designed for a personal, CGM system and facile decision-making related to diabetes management (Scheme 14D) (https://www.diabetesnet.c). The Dexcom G4 PLATINUM device is calibrated twice daily by using the patient's fingerprint and offers glucose concentration levels every 5 min for 7 days. Data can be downloaded using the Dexcom CLARITY web-based software for simple reading of data by individuals. Furthermore, the FreeStyle Libre device (Abbott Diabetes Care Inc., Alameda, CA, USA) was formulated with auto-calibration (i.e. no need of personal calibration) for direct monitoring of diabetes (Scheme 14E) (Schierenbeck et al., 2017). This FreeStyle Libre device, composed of a small sensor (about 2 cm in diameter) placed under the upper arm skin, detects glucose level every 15 min for 14 days. However, FreeStyle Libre is not systematically permitted to be used for children under the age of 18, pregnant women and dialysis patients. The Eversense XL System was designed to provide simple and reliable CGM up to 180 days. In this sensor design, implantable, removable and rechargeable smart transmitter, and compatible smartphone app are correlated for accurate and on-time analyses (Scheme 14F) (https://global.eversensed). The Medtronic 670G device is used for CGM analysis and for delivery of diabetes-related products. This device affords great possibility to regulate the delivery of normal insulin rate every 5 min under automatic control mode (Scheme 14G) (https://www.diabetesdaily, 2018). The quality of these electronic devices for CGM is undisputable, but novel developments of nano-electronic devices with compact size, low-cost analysis and on-time and accurate data acquisition are still demanded to improve public healthcare and to reduce mortality (Quinault et al., 2016; van den Berghe et al., 2001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion and future outlook
ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing public demands of early detection and self-monitoring of diabetes, the development of visual, easy-to-use and wearable sensor electronics, physiological fluid sampling, readout data analysis and acquisition has gained considerable interest. Our comprehensive review bridges the practical assessment gap between optical bio-visualisation assays, disposable test strips, sensor array designs and the full integration into flexible skin-based or contact lens devices with on-site wireless signal transmission.

In-depth study, this review sheds insight into the recent, considerable growth associated with the development of nano/microscale architecture-based optical biosensor strips and devices for simple, cost-effective visualisation and real-time detection of glucose. The emerging impacts of active nano/microscale materials into optical and portable biosensor arrays have promising prospects for direct bio-visualisation sensing systems and their integrations into flexible skin-based or contact lens biosensor array devices. To date, nano/microscale architecture sensor designs featuring high efficiency, high quality and reliability and periodicity have attracted attention for disposable test strips and wearable biosensing technology. For future personalised healthcare, development of a wearable biosensor-integrated smartphone for the on-time visual screening, point-of-use monitoring and rapid diagnosis of diabetes at early stages is urgently needed.

Growing developments of optical whispering gallery mode (WGM)-incorporated plasmonic nanostructures have been recently reported (Kang et al., 2017; Frustaci and Vollmer, 2019; Zhang et al., 2018b). Sensing device systems are based on the integration leverage of hybrid confining plasmonic–photonic biosensors for strong signalling and detection of single molecules, such as glucose. Hybrid WGM biosensors have shown promise to be technologically relevant for sensitive visualisation, selective signalling and precise identification assays of glucose levels in physiological fluid samples. To improve the promising prospects of WGM–hybrid biosensors, a molecularly selective device with low-cost analysis, reliable and rapid measurements, dynamic signal processing and multimodal sensing integrations into wearable medical devices is necessary. Therefore, the future challenges and directions should integrate the multifunctional WGM hybrid plasmonic–photonic-colorimetric readouts into mechanically flexible skin-based wearable sensors for the in situ analysis of body physiological fluids. Such developments of wearable optical-WGM multi-sensor array settings are expected to offer low-cost, ultra-high sensitivity, smart portability and wirelessly detectable transmission with higher sensitivity compared with the single WGM sensing array.

The advances of the wearable sensing technology open insights of easy-to-use, portable and optical glucose biosensors for suppressing the health risks associated with the surgical insulin therapy of diabetes. Nano/micro physiological biosensor chipsets with circuits that have high electronic transmittance, robust structural stabilities, readable output signalling and visible tracking without power consumption are of particular interest for the synthesis of wearable electronic devices. Optical skin or contact lens biosensors have become an important tool in real-time health indication of patients with diabetes. Accordingly, a series of flexible and wearable devices based on nano/microscale biomolecular sensor technology is vital for reliable healthcare information and non-invasive monitoring. Fully modulated integrations of optical, physiological biosensors into electronic device modalities, such as smartphones, are substantial to meet the demands of rapid visualisation readout analysis, real life-self-powered monitoring and early detection of diabetes. Development of continuous, applicable and dynamic visualisation readouts and battery-free wireless systems for online data acquisition and transmission is a key challenge for the large-scale manufacture and commercialisation of wearable medical sensors for patients with diabetes.

Improvement of clinical settings and full integration of skin-safely soft, transparent and stretchable nanoscale materials on wearable medical devices for wireless signal detection and accurate modulation into smartphones are urgently needed to improve the self-care management of patients with diabetes. For prolonged periods of indoor and outdoor clinical diagnostics, a substantial management of optical wearable skin-based or contact lens sensors is needed to control the continuous and self-monitoring of and provide appropriate treatment for patients with diabetes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this review article.
